space.notes: Sorts in-place, but requires expected $\Theta(\log n)$ space for the recursion call stack. In the worst-case (unbalanced partitions), the space complexity can degrade to $O(n)$ if tail-call optimization is not implemented.

documentation.description: Quicksort is an efficient, in-place sorting algorithm that uses the divide-and-conquer paradigm. It partitions an array into two subarrays around a pivot element and recursively sorts them.
how_it_works: Divide: Select a pivot element from the array and partition the elements such that all elements less than or equal to the pivot are placed to its left, and all strictly greater elements are placed to its right.
Conquer: Recursively call the quicksort algorithm on the two subarrays (left of the pivot and right of the pivot).
Combine: Because the subarrays are sorted in place, no explicit combination step is needed to merge them. The entire array is sorted.
applications: It is widely used as a general-purpose sorting algorithm in standard libraries (e.g., C++ std::sort utilizes Introsort, which is based on Quicksort) due to its excellent average-case performance and small constant factors.
It works exceptionally well in virtual-memory environments because of its good cache locality and sequential memory access patterns.
pitfalls: The worst-case time complexity of $\Theta(n^2)$ occurs when the array is already sorted or reverse sorted and a naive pivot strategy (like choosing the last element) is used.
To mitigate worst-case scenarios, a randomized pivot selection (Randomized Quicksort) or the Median-of-3 method should be implemented.

comparisons.reason: Merge sort provides a guaranteed worst-case time complexity of $\Theta(n \log n)$ and is stable, unlike quicksort. However, quicksort operates in-place requiring only $\Theta(\log n)$ space, and generally outperforms merge sort in practice due to smaller constant factors and superior cache locality.

implementations.snippet:
QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
  if p < r
    q = PARTITION(A, p, r)
    QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
    QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)

PARTITION(A, p, r)
  x = A[r]
  i = p - 1
  for j = p to r - 1
    if A[j] <= x
      i = i + 1
      exchange A[i] with A[j]
  exchange A[i + 1] with A[r]
  return i + 1
implementations.explanation: This pseudocode implements the Lomuto partition scheme using 0-indexed bounds. p is the starting index and r is the ending index (inclusive).
A real-world C++ implementation operating on std::vector<int>. It mimics the 0-indexed pseudocode perfectly.

citations.authors: Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, Clifford Stein

space.notes: Requires additional $\Theta(n)$ space for temporary arrays during the merge step.

documentation.description: Merge sort is an efficient, stable, comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses the divide-and-conquer paradigm. It divides the array into halves, sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves.
how_it_works: Divide: Divide the n-element sequence to be sorted into two subsequences of n/2 elements each.
Conquer: Sort the two subsequences recursively using merge sort.
Combine: Merge the two sorted subsequences to produce the sorted answer.
applications: Used when stability is required (equal elements maintain their relative order).
External sorting where data doesn't fit in memory.
Linked list sorting (can be implemented with O(1) extra space).
pitfalls: Higher space complexity compared to Quicksort or Heapsort.

comparisons.reason: Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) even in the worst case, whereas Quick Sort can degrade to O(n^2). However, Quick Sort is usually faster in practice due to lower overhead.

implementations.snippet:
MERGE-SORT(A, p, r)
  if p < r
    q = floor((p + r) / 2)
    MERGE-SORT(A, p, q)
    MERGE-SORT(A, q + 1, r)
    MERGE(A, p, q, r)
implementations.explanation: Classic recursive structure for Merge Sort.

citations.authors: Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, Clifford Stein

metadata.title: Binary Search
metadata.subtitle: Search a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half

space.notes: Iterative implementation uses constant space. Recursive uses $O(\log n)$ stack space.

documentation.description: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an item from a sorted list of items. It works by repeatedly dividing in half the portion of the list that could contain the item, until you've narrowed down the possible locations to just one.
how_it_works: Compare the target value to the middle element of the array.
If they are equal, the search is successful and the index is returned.
If the target is less than the middle element, repeat the search on the left half.
If the target is greater than the middle element, repeat the search on the right half.
applications: Searching in sorted arrays or lists.
pitfalls: Integer overflow when calculating mid: `(low + high) / 2` should be `low + (high - low) / 2`.

comparisons.reason: Linear search is O(n) while Binary search is O(log n). For large datasets, Binary search is significantly faster.

metadata.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
metadata.subtitle: Single-source shortest path algorithm for graphs with non-negative edge weights

space.notes: Requires space for distance array and priority queue.

documentation.description: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a starting node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights.
how_it_works: Initialize distances to all nodes as infinity, except the source node (0).
Insert all nodes into a priority queue based on distance.
While the queue is not empty, extract the node with the minimum distance.
For each neighbor, update its distance if a shorter path is found (Relaxation).
pitfalls: Does not work with negative edge weights (use Bellman-Ford instead).
Performance depends heavily on the priority queue implementation (Fibonacci heap vs Binary heap).

comparisons.reason: BFS works for shortest paths only in unweighted graphs. Dijkstra handles weighted graphs.

implementations.explanation: Python implementation using heapq for the priority queue.

citations.source_name: A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs

metadata.title: Fibonacci (DP)
metadata.subtitle: Calculating Fibonacci numbers using Dynamic Programming (Memoization/Tabulation)

space.notes: Tabulation uses O(n) space. Optimized iterative version uses O(1).

documentation.description: Calculating the nth Fibonacci number efficiently using Dynamic Programming to avoid redundant calculations of subproblems.
how_it_works: Identify the recurrence relation: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2).
Memoization (Top-Down): Store results of subproblems in a map/array during recursion.
Tabulation (Bottom-Up): Fill an array from 0 to n sequentially.
pitfalls: Plain recursion is O(2^n) - extremely slow for large n.
Integer overflow happens very quickly for Fibonacci numbers (needs BigInt for large n).

comparisons.reason: DP reduces the complexity from exponential O(2^n) to linear O(n).

citations.authors: Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, Clifford Stein
